perturbation.original_text: Payments will be due in US dollars within 30 days after delivery, or when agreed after delivery of installments or the receipt of invoice by Dragon Systems, which ever is later.
perturbation.changed_text: Payments will be due in US dollars within 90 days after delivery, or when agreed after delivery of installments or the receipt of invoice by Dragon Systems, which ever is later.
perturbation.explanation: Extended payment terms to 90 days may violate prompt payment laws if they unreasonably delay payments for goods/services, especially if MMI is a small business.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 149, § 29E sets requirements for prompt payment to contractors and subcontractors on public construction projects in Massachusetts. While this contract isn't for construction, similar prompt payment principles may apply or influence the interpretation of reasonable payment terms, particularly if MMI is a smaller vendor. Extending payment to 90 days may be considered unreasonable and a violation of implied duties of good faith and fair dealing, especially considering that the text says Dragon Systems shall be entitled to deduct from or set off against any sums which Dragon Systems may be liable to pay to MMI any amounts owed by MMI.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XXI, Chapter 149, Section 29E Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XXI LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Chapter 149 LABOR AND INDUSTRIES Section 29E Construction contracts; reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments and increases in contract price; payment conditioned upon receipt of payment from third party; requirement to continue performance of construction without payment Section 29E Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 29E: Construction contracts; reasonable time periods for periodic progress payments and increases in contract price; payment conditioned upon receipt of payment from third party; requirement to continue performance of construction without payment Section 29E. (a) As used in this section the following words shall have the following meanings, unless the context clearly requires otherwise: ''Contract for construction'', a contract for which a lien may be established under sections 2 or 4 of chapter 254 on a project for which the person whose contract with the project owner has an original contract price of $3,000,000 or more; provided, however, this shall not include projects containing or designed to contain at least 1 but not more than 4 dwelling units. ''Insolvent'', insolvent as defined under federal bankruptcy law or being a debtor in a proceeding commenced under federal bankruptcy law or under the corresponding law of another country, or being a debtor in a receivership proceeding or having made an assignment for the benefit of creditors. (b) A communication required in this section to be in writing may be submitted in electronic form and by electronic means. (c) Every contract for construction shall provide reasonable time periods within which: (i) a person seeking payment under the contract shall submit written applications for periodic progress payments; (ii) the person receiving the application
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment terms from 30 to 90 days. The Law Explanation suggests that extending payment terms to 90 days may be considered unreasonable, potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing. The Perturbation Explanation notes that extended payment terms to 90 days may violate prompt payment laws if they unreasonably delay payments for goods/services, especially if MMI is a small business. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses prompt payment for construction contracts under M.G.L. c. 149, § 29E, the Law Explanation draws a parallel, suggesting similar principles may influence the interpretation of reasonable payment terms in this non-construction contract. The snippet defines 'contract for construction' but the principle for prompt payments may be applicable beyond construction contracts. Since the original text was 30 days but was changed to 90, it sets a deadline that is likely to violate federal or state law. This aligns with the definition of inconsistencies legal contradiction. Therefore, the extended payment term creates a potential contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Should MMI not supply the Services as agreed or should the Services become defective within 6 months from their delivery to Dragon Systems, Dragon Systems may at its option require MMI to complete or re-perform the Services within a reasonable period of time, rescind the contract or refuse payment of the compensation in part or in total, notwithstanding any damage claims.
perturbation.changed_text: Should MMI not supply the Services as agreed or should the Services become defective within 1 month from their delivery to Dragon Systems, Dragon Systems may at its option require MMI to complete or re-perform the Services within a reasonable period of time, rescind the contract or refuse payment of the compensation in part or in total, notwithstanding any damage claims.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the warranty period to 1 month could be deemed unconscionable and violate consumer protection laws, as it unreasonably limits the buyer's rights to redress for defective products. The original text of 6 months is more common.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 prohibits unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce. While this is a B2B contract, drastically reducing the warranty period to 1 month may be seen as an unfair or deceptive practice, especially if the product is complex or prone to defects. By significantly reducing the warranty period, it contradicts the intent and purpose of providing reasonable consumer protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93A, Section 2 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93A REGULATION OF BUSINESS PRACTICES FOR CONSUMERS PROTECTION Section 2 Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 2: Unfair practices; legislative intent; rules and regulations Section 2. (a) Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful. (b) It is the intent of the legislature that in construing paragraph (a) of this section in actions brought under sections four, nine and eleven, the courts will be guided by the interpretations given by the Federal Trade Commission and the Federal Courts to section 5(a)(1) of the Federal Trade Commission Act (15 U.S.C. 45(a)(1)), as from time to time amended. (c) The attorney general may make rules and regulations interpreting the provisions of subsection 2(a) of this chapter. perturbation.justification: The modified contract reduces the warranty period from 6 months to 1 month, which the Law Explanation suggests could be an unfair practice under M.G.L. c. 93A, § 2 if it's deemed an unfair or deceptive act, especially if the product is complex. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful'. The Perturbation Explanation states this warranty reduction 'unreasonably limits the buyer's rights to redress for defective products'. Because the reduced warranty period may be seen as an unfair practice and violates consumer protection laws it creates a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Dragon may terminate this agreement without cause by giving sixty (60) days written notice to MMI.
perturbation.changed_text: Dragon may terminate this agreement without cause by giving fifteen (15) days written notice to MMI.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 15 days could be viewed as a breach of good faith and fair dealing, especially if MMI needs more time to adjust operations or find alternative clients.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (Massachusetts Contract Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not explicitly stated in a statute, Massachusetts law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Reducing the termination notice to 15 days could be deemed a violation of this covenant if it significantly harms MMI's business operations and provides insufficient time for them to mitigate losses associated with the contract termination. This is further supported by Dragon System's right to deduct from or set off against any sums which Dragon Systems may be liable to pay to MMI any amounts owed by MMI. This is unreasonable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 60 to 15 days. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Massachusetts law, especially if it harms MMI's business operations. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Section 1-304 of the General Laws, states that every contract imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Reducing the notice period significantly could be seen as acting in bad faith, creating a legal contradiction because the altered term could be deemed unenforceable.
